8:
"Behold now, I have two daughters which have not known man; let me, I pray you, bring them out unto you, and do ye to them as is good in your eyes: only unto these men do nothing; for therefore came they under the shadow of my roof." The witnesses note Lot offering his daughters to the mob — the crux of his act. On "I have two daughters which have not known man; let me... bring them out unto you," interpreters expound and judge Lot's offer. The reformed Gill and the dominant moral judgment hold it a grave fault, not to be excused: Lot, in his distress, offered his two virgin daughters to the lust of the mob, to save his guests; the proposal was sinful — a man may not commit one sin (exposing his daughters to abomination) to prevent another; the offer, though springing from the pressure of the moment and the duty of hospitality, was a great fault, the choosing of a lesser evil being no warrant. Some (with Chrysostom and others) judge it the more grievously; others note the mitigating circumstance — the sacred, paramount duty of protecting guests in the customs of the East, under which Lot felt bound to shield the strangers at any cost; the rights of hospitality regarded among the ancients (and still in Arabia) as inviolable; Lot acting under that overwhelming sense of duty, though still wrongly.

On "do ye to them as is good in your eyes: only unto these men do nothing," the grammatical-historical and wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the motive. Clarke and Ellicott note that Lot's motive was to protect his guests ("only unto these men do nothing; for therefore came they under the shadow of my roof") — the sacred obligation of the host to shield those who had taken refuge under his roof; Lot, bound by the law of hospitality, sought to save his guests at the terrible cost of his daughters; the motive (the protection of the guests) explains, but does not justify, the offer; the act remains a fault, however understandable.

On "under the shadow of my roof," the grammatical-historical note the duty: the guests had come "under the shadow of my roof" — the host's sacred duty to protect them; Lot's overwhelming sense of this obligation; the pressure that led him to the wrong proposal; the strait that confused the righteous man. The verse displays Lot offering his daughters to the mob.

On "brimstone and fire from the LORD out of heaven," interpreters expound the repetition. The dominant Christian reading (the puritan Henry and many) takes "the LORD... from the LORD" as marking two divine Persons: the LORD (the Son, on earth, who had been with Abraham, ch. 18) rained the fire from the LORD (the Father) out of heaven; God the Son from God the Father; the repetition a proof-text for the plurality of Persons in the Godhead (the Father and the Son both called Jehovah); the Son, the executor of judgment, raining the fire from the Father in heaven. A second reading takes "from the LORD" as a Hebrew idiom — a repetition for emphasis (the LORD from himself, that is, from his own immediate power and presence), not necessarily denoting two Persons; the emphatic phrase marking that the judgment came directly from the LORD out of heaven, his own act.

On Moab, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: the origin of Moab (and Ammon, v.38) in the shameful incident, yet the overruling providence of God — that even from this sad origin came peoples in the purposes of God (Moab giving Ruth, the line of Christ); the grace of God overruling the sin; the sad yet providentially-used origin of the nation. The verse displays the birth of Moab.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the birth — "the firstborn bare a son, and called his name Moab," the elder's son, named Moab (Keil, Gill); "the father of the Moabites unto this day," the ancestor of the Moabites, kin yet often enemies of Israel (Deuteronomy 2:9, Clarke, Gill); Moab's line giving Ruth, an ancestress of Christ (Ruth 4:13-22; Matthew 1:5, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the born Moab. The verse marks the son: Moab. The verse displays the firstborn: his mother. The verse displays the Moabites: his nation. The verse displays the providence: of God. The birth displays the origin of the Moabites from the elder daughter. The verse displays the Moab: born. The verse displays the nation: descended. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The birth of Moab, father of the Moabites, displays the origin of the nation: the firstborn bore Moab, the father of the Moabites — the son of the elder daughter, ancestor of the Moabites (kin yet often enemies of Israel), and, through Ruth, of the line of Christ (Matthew 1:5), God's providence overruling the shameful origin. The verse displays the born Moab, with Ben-ammi (v.38).
38:
"And the younger, she also bare a son, and called his name Benammi: the same is the father of the children of Ammon unto this day." The witnesses note the birth of Ben-ammi, father of the Ammonites. On "the younger, she also bare a son, and called his name Benammi," the grammatical voices note the birth. Gill and Clarke note that "the younger, she also bare a son, and called his name Benammi (son of my people/kindred); the same is the father of the children of Ammon" — the younger daughter bore a son, named Ben-ammi; the ancestor of the Ammonites; the son of the younger daughter; the second of the two nations from this incident.

On "the father of the children of Ammon unto this day," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the nation. Clarke and Gill note that Ben-ammi was "the father of the children of Ammon" — the ancestor of the Ammonite nation (settled east of Moab); a people, like Moab, of mixed relation to Israel — often enemies (Judges 11; 1 Samuel 11; 2 Samuel 10), yet kin (Deuteronomy 2:19, the land of Ammon not to be taken); the origin of the Ammonites in this incident.

On the two nations, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the close: the Moabites and Ammonites, sprung from Lot's daughters, were peoples on the borders of Israel, kin yet often hostile; the sad origin of both in the shameful incident; the providence of God in the rise of the nations; the close of the history of Lot (a sad end for the man who chose the plain, 13:10-11); the chapter ending with the origin of these peoples. The verse displays the birth of Ben-ammi, father of the Ammonites.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the birth — "the younger... bare a son, and called his name Benammi," the younger's son, named Ben-ammi (Gill, Clarke); "the father of the children of Ammon unto this day," the ancestor of the Ammonites, kin yet often enemies (Deuteronomy 2:19, Clarke, Gill); the sad origin of both nations, the close of Lot's history (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the born Ben-ammi. The verse marks the son: Ben-ammi. The verse displays the younger: his mother. The verse displays the Ammonites: his nation. The verse displays the Lot: his sad end. The birth displays the origin of the Ammonites from the younger daughter. The verse displays the Ben-ammi: born. The verse displays the nation: descended. The verse displays the history: closed. The birth of Ben-ammi, father of the Ammonites, displays the origin of the second nation and the close of Lot's history: the younger bore Ben-ammi, the father of the Ammonites — the son of the younger daughter, ancestor of the Ammonites (kin yet often enemies of Israel), the sad close of the history of Lot, who chose the plain (13:10-11). The verse closes the chapter, before the sojourn at Gerar (ch.20).
